steps.label: 5.12 Theorem Limit Comparison Test
steps.bodyHtml:
Let \(a_n\), \(b_n\geq0\) for all \(n\geq1\).

i. If \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}=L\neq0\), then \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n\) and \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}b_n\) both converge or both diverge

ii. If \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}=0\) and \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}b_n\) converges, then \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n\) converges

iii. If \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}=\infty\) and \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}b_n\) diverges, then \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n\) diverges

steps.label: Warning
steps.bodyHtml:
i. If \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}=0\) and \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}b_n\) diverges, the limit comparison test gives no information about the behaviour of \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n\)

ii. If \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}=\infty\) and \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}b_n\) converges, the limit comparison test gives no information about the behavior of \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n\)

Example: Consider the series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\) and \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^2}\). You know their behavior because they are p-series.

steps.label: Exercises
steps.bodyHtml:
Use the limit comparison test for the next series

i. \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}+1}\)

ii. \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{2^n+1}{3^n}\)

iii. \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{ln(n)}{n^2}\)
